Direct yourself to the worship of God, and take time to prepare yourself for it. Keep your thoughts from wandering: keep your affections from being drawn toward wrong things. We should avoid meaningless repetitions; lengthy prayers are not condemned here, but those that have no meaning. How often our wandering thoughts make attending religious services little better than the sacrifice of fools! Many words and hurried ones used in prayer show foolishness in the heart, low opinions of God, and careless consideration for your own souls.

When a person makes commitments rashly, he allows his mouth to cause his body to sin. The case assumes a man coming to the priest, claiming that his vow was made hastily, and that it would be wrong to fulfill it. Such mockery of God would bring divine displeasure, which might ruin what is thus improperly kept. We are to control the fear of people. Set God before you; then, if you see the oppression of the poor, you will not find fault with God’s providence; nor think poorly of the institution of government, when you see its purposes perverted; nor of religion, when you see it does not protect people from suffering wrong. But though oppressors may be secure, God will hold them accountable.

The goodness of Providence is more evenly distributed than appears to a careless observer. The king needs the basic necessities of life, and the poor share them; they enjoy their simple meal better than he enjoys his luxuries. There are bodily desires that silver itself cannot satisfy, much less can worldly abundance satisfy spiritual desires. The more men have, the better house they must keep, the more servants they must employ, the more guests they must entertain, and the more obligations they will have. The sleep of the laborer is sweet, not only because he is tired, but because he has little to worry about that would disturb his sleep. The sleep of the diligent Christian, and his long sleep, are sweet; having spent himself and his time in service to God, he can cheerfully rest in God as his Rest. But those who have everything else often fail to secure a good night’s sleep; their abundance disrupts their rest. Riches do harm, and draw away the heart from God and duty. People do harm with their riches, not only indulging their own desires, but oppressing others, and dealing harshly with them. They will see that they have labored for nothing, when, at death, they find that the profit of their labor is all gone like the wind; they do not know where it has gone. How poorly the covetous person bears the calamities of life! He does not grieve to repent, but is angry at God’s providence, angry at everyone around him; which doubles his suffering.

Life is God's gift. We must not view our calling as a drudgery, but take pleasure in the work where God places us. A cheerful spirit is a great blessing; it makes tasks easy and troubles light. Having used riches properly, a person will remember the days of his past life with pleasure. The way Solomon refers to God as the Giver, both of life and its pleasures, shows that they ought to be received and used consistently with his will, and to his glory. Let this passage recommend to all the kind words of the merciful Redeemer, "Do not work for food that spoils, but for food that lasts forever." Christ is the Bread of life, the only nourishment for the soul. All are invited to partake of this heavenly provision.
